name: SLB Limited

profile.description: SLB Limited (formerly Schlumberger) is the world's largest oilfield services company, providing technology and integrated solutions across the energy value chain in more than 100 countries. The company operates through four divisions: Digital & Integration, Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, and Production Systems. SLB completed the $7.8B acquisition of ChampionX in July 2025, expanding into production chemicals. The company is driving digital transformation through its Delfi cognitive E&P platform and Lumi AI platform, while investing in energy transition technologies including carbon capture and new energy systems.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): SLB completed the transformative $7.8B ChampionX acquisition in July 2025, expanding into production chemicals and adding $3.51B in quarterly Production Systems revenue (up 23% YoY) [1][2]. The company also launched the Lumi AI platform in September 2024 and announced Autonomous Directional Drilling in early 2026, which uses AI to adjust drilling paths in real time and reduces drilling time by up to 30% [3]. However, organic revenue declined 7% YoY excluding ChampionX, signaling that the core business transformation is still in progress rather than delivering broad growth. AI Deployment (1/2): SLB's Lumi data and AI platform and Tela agentic AI assistant are shipping to customers, with the Delfi cognitive E&P platform utilized by nearly all major National Oil Companies [3]. Autonomous Directional Drilling is deployed commercially with measurable 30% time savings, but specific AI-driven revenue or cost-savings figures have not been disclosed [4]. Market Share (1/2): SLB is the largest oilfield services company globally with $35.7B in 2025 revenue, but market share has not materially expanded — international revenue declined 7% and North America declined 8% YoY in Q1 2026 amid Middle East disruptions and NOC project recalibrations [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Lumi AI platform, Tela agentic AI assistant, and Autonomous Directional Drilling represent a clear digital pipeline, and the company is developing data center cooling infrastructure leveraging thermal management expertise [3][4]. However, specific next-gen product launch dates and revenue visibility are limited. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending of approximately $714-731M annually represents ~2.0% of revenue, which is at the high end for oilfield services peers like Halliburton (~1.5%) and Baker Hughes (~1.8%) [5].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): SLB's stated purpose is 'to create amazing technology that unlocks access to energy for the benefit of all,' which is clear and connects the company's work to global energy access — a genuine societal need [6]. It differentiates from generic oilfield services positioning, but the mission has not been validated by decades of consistent execution in the Sinek sense — SLB rebranded from Schlumberger only in 2023, and the energy transition framing is relatively new. Community Impact (1/2): The Schlumberger Foundation, established in 1954, runs the Faculty for the Future program supporting women from developing countries pursuing STEM doctoral degrees, demonstrating distinctive and long-running philanthropic commitment aligned with STEM education [7]. However, specific annual giving amounts and broader community investment metrics beyond the Foundation are not readily available. Employee Retention (1/2): SLB employs 109,000 people globally with a stable workforce (down modestly from 110,000 in 2024), and CEO Le Peuch emphasizes 'collective creativity' and team empowerment [8]. However, no specific turnover, tenure, or retention data is publicly available, and the oilfield services industry generally experiences higher turnover due to cyclical project-based work. Insider Buying (1/2): Insider activity in 2025-2026 has been dominated by selling — all 7 recent insider transactions totaling $10.4M were sales, including a $3.0M sale by EVP Steve Gassen in May 2026 [9]. While these may represent planned diversification, there is no meaningful net insider buying to signal conviction.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on financial metrics. Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B grew 3% YoY (boosted by ChampionX), though organic revenue declined 7% [1]. Debt-to-equity of 42.5% is well below the 70% target, reflecting a conservatively leveraged balance sheet after absorbing the $7.8B ChampionX acquisition. ROE of 14.1% narrowly misses the 15% target but remains solid for oilfield services [5]. Forward P/E of 16.6 is comfortably below the 20 target, indicating reasonable valuation relative to expected earnings recovery. Profit margin of 9.3% is below the 20% target, dragged down by the lower-margin ChampionX production chemicals business and Middle East operational disruptions that compressed Q1 adjusted EBITDA by 12% YoY [1]. Revenue growth 5-year CAGR of 6.0% (2021-2025) reflects strong post-COVID recovery but falls short of the 10% target. Dividend metrics are a strength: 32 consecutive years of payments since 1994, 5 consecutive years of growth, payout ratio of 47.5%, and 5-year dividend growth CAGR of 5.4% [10].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (5/9): SLB's core business is deeply rooted in physical-world operations — drilling wells, deploying subsea equipment, managing reservoir pressure, and maintaining production infrastructure — which AI structurally cannot replace [4]. The Well Construction ($2.8B/quarter) and Production Systems ($3.5B/quarter) divisions involve hardware, engineering, and field operations that require physical presence. However, SLB's high-margin Digital & Integration segment ($3.3B annual revenue) faces direct AI disruption risk: AI-powered alternatives including large language models capable of interpreting seismic data, well logs, and production histories are reducing the complexity premium that justified SLB's consulting and software pricing [4]. Analysts have assigned an AI Margin Pressure Score of 6/10, noting that SLB is simultaneously the primary developer of oilfield AI tools and the company most exposed to their commoditizing effect — by building Lumi and Delfi, SLB enables operators to perform subsurface analysis in-house, potentially cannibalizing its own interpretation services [4]. AI Opportunity (1/1): SLB generates meaningful AI-related revenue through its Digital & Integration segment, Lumi AI platform, and Tela agentic AI assistant deployed across nearly all major National Oil Companies [3]. The Autonomous Directional Drilling system delivers quantifiable value (30% drilling time reduction), and the company is expanding into AI data center cooling infrastructure, leveraging thermal management expertise from decades of downhole engineering [3][4].

infinite_game.summary: SLB under Olivier Le Peuch is evolving from a cyclical oilfield services provider toward an infinite-game technology company, but the transformation is incomplete. The rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB in 2023, the articulation of a purpose centered on 'energy for the benefit of all,' and the strategic pivot toward digital, AI, and energy transition technologies all signal infinite-game intent. However, the purpose lacks decades of validated execution, the 2020 dividend cut was a finite-game capitulation to near-term pressure, and the company's competitive language still emphasizes 'performance' and market leadership rather than enduring purpose.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): SLB's purpose — 'to create amazing technology that unlocks access to energy for the benefit of all' — is specific, thoughtful, and connects the company's engineering expertise to the globally significant challenge of energy access [6]. Leadership references it consistently: Le Peuch's THRIVE Conference keynote framed AI and digital investments as extensions of this purpose, and the company's sustainability report positions decarbonization as integral to the mission, not separate from it. Strategic decisions can be traced to it — the Lumi AI platform, Autonomous Drilling, and energy transition investments all align with 'unlocking energy through technology.' However, this purpose framing is relatively new (SLB rebranded only in 2023), and it has not yet been validated through a multi-decade track record of consistent execution under this articulation. The company operated for 97 years as 'Schlumberger' with a more traditional services identity.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): SLB's 109,000-person workforce has remained stable through the post-COVID recovery (86K in 2021 to 111K peak in 2024, now 109K), suggesting organizational stability rather than disruptive layoff cycles [8]. Le Peuch's leadership style emphasizes 'empowering teams to excel' and 'collective creativity,' and his 35+ year career at SLB beginning as an electrical engineer in 1987 demonstrates deep institutional commitment [3]. The company's global footprint across 100+ countries with employees representing nearly 200 nationalities requires cross-cultural trust. However, no specific employee retention metrics, voluntary turnover rates, or evidence of exceptional psychological safety are publicly available — and oilfield services historically involves project-based, rotational work that creates inherent team instability.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): SLB's competitive language under Le Peuch consistently frames the company as a 'partner of choice' and 'global technology company' rather than positioning against Halliburton or Baker Hughes in adversarial terms [3][6]. Le Peuch's CERAWeek 2026 remarks focused on industry-wide digital transformation and energy transition collaboration, not market share warfare. The company's open acknowledgment that AI tools could commoditize its own high-margin services — and its choice to build and deploy those tools anyway through Lumi and Delfi — reflects an infinite-game approach to competition: improving the industry rather than protecting a position.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): SLB has meaningfully evolved its business model under Le Peuch: divesting underperforming assets upon his 2019 appointment, taking $12B+ in impairment charges to reset the portfolio, rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB in 2023, and acquiring ChampionX for $7.8B to expand into production chemicals [2]. The company has built a Digital & Integration segment from near-zero to $3.3B in annual revenue and is investing in energy transition technologies including carbon capture. These are real strategic pivots, not cosmetic changes. However, the transformation is evolutionary rather than transformative in the Sinek sense — SLB's core remains oilfield services, and the company has not abandoned a profitable legacy business in service of a fundamentally different future the way Apple abandoned the Mac for the iPhone.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Le Peuch demonstrated courage upon becoming CEO in 2019 by immediately divesting underperforming assets and taking massive impairment charges to reposition the company, choosing long-term health over short-term earnings optics [3]. The $7.8B ChampionX acquisition in 2025 was a significant strategic bet on production chemicals and lifecycle services. SLB's investment in AI tools that could cannibalize its own high-margin consulting business (Lumi, Autonomous Drilling) shows willingness to disrupt itself. However, the 2020 dividend cut from $0.50 to $0.125 per quarter — while financially prudent during the oil price crash — was a capitulation to near-term pressure rather than a courageous long-term investment, and some strategic moves (energy transition investments) have been cautious relative to the company's rhetoric.

investment_thesis: SLB is the world's largest oilfield services company with $35.7B in 2025 revenue, a conservatively leveraged balance sheet (D/E 42.5%), and a forward P/E of 16.6 that prices in near-term headwinds while offering upside if oil markets stabilize [1][5]. The ChampionX acquisition added $3.5B in quarterly Production Systems revenue and diversified the company into higher-recurring production chemicals, while the Digital & Integration segment generates premium-margin revenue through the Lumi AI platform and Delfi cognitive E&P environment deployed across nearly all major National Oil Companies [1][3]. SLB's Autonomous Directional Drilling technology — reducing drilling time by 30% — and its emerging data center cooling business represent tangible AI-era growth vectors [4]. The dividend yields 2.2% with a sustainable 47.5% payout ratio, backed by 32 consecutive years of payments and a 5-year growth CAGR of 5.4% [10].
thesis_continued: The primary concern is that SLB faces a challenging demand environment: Q1 2026 organic revenue declined 7% YoY excluding ChampionX, international revenue fell 7%, and North America fell 8% as Middle East logistics bottlenecks and NOC project recalibrations compressed activity [1]. Adjusted EBITDA declined 12% YoY to $1.77B, and GAAP EPS of $0.50 was down 14%, suggesting that the ChampionX acquisition is masking organic deterioration [1]. SLB also faces a structural tension in its highest-margin Digital & Integration business: by building and deploying AI tools (Lumi, Delfi, Tela) that enable operators to perform subsurface analysis in-house, the company is potentially commoditizing the interpretation and consulting services that historically commanded premium pricing [4]. Oil price volatility remains an overarching risk — every $10/bbl decline in Brent reduces upstream capex budgets that directly drive SLB's revenue, and the company's 2025 revenue already declined 1.6% amid OPEC+ uncertainty [5].
key_catalysts: ChampionX integration synergies expected to deliver cost savings and cross-selling opportunities across SLB's 109,000-person global footprint, with Production Systems already contributing 23% YoY revenue growth in Q1 2026

Lumi AI platform and Autonomous Directional Drilling adoption accelerating across major NOCs, with 30% drilling time reductions creating measurable operator savings that drive digital segment revenue growth
Emerging data center cooling and power infrastructure business leveraging SLB's thermal management expertise to capture AI-era capex, diversifying revenue beyond traditional upstream oil and gas
key_risks: Organic revenue declining 7% YoY in Q1 2026 excluding ChampionX, with Middle East logistics disruptions and NOC project recalibrations signaling demand softness that could persist through 2026 if OPEC+ uncertainties continue

AI commoditization of SLB's high-margin Digital & Integration segment as operators deploy in-house AI tools for seismic interpretation and well log analysis, compressing the complexity premium that justified premium pricing

Oil price sensitivity remains acute — a sustained Brent decline below $65/bbl would trigger upstream capex cuts that directly reduce SLB's revenue pipeline, particularly in the international markets that represent 80%+ of revenue
